g2u03.w.century.note: s 'She was dressed like a girl from the 19th ___.' leaves the passive 'was dressed (like)' unglossed. The bank teaches the noun 'dress' (= Kleid, this unit) and 'to get dressed' (g2-u01), not the passive 'be dressed like' (= verkleidet/angezogen wie). Sibling w.superheroine glosses the exact parallel chunk 'dressed up as (= verkleidet als)', so by the corpus's own standard this carrier wants the same gloss. The headword stays gettable ('One hundred years - a very long time'; '19th ___'), so the student is not blocked.

g2u03.w.front-window.note: d 'The glass in a building that you look out of, into the street' stacks a relative clause with a stranded preposition ('that you look out of') plus an appended fragment ('into the street') — parsing beyond A1+ even though every token is taught — and uses 'glass' in the window-pane sense (taught only as the drinking glass, g1-u07; German 'Glas' is polysemous the same way, so meaning survives). The transparent headword and the clean carrier s ('We put some vampire stickers on his ___.') rescue the item; only the definition syntax outruns the level.

g2u03.w.guys.note: d 'You can call a group of friends or children this.' uses 'call' in the untaught naming sense (= jemanden so nennen) — g1-u06 teaches 'to call' as rufen/anrufen — inside a call + OBJECT + OBJECT pattern with cataphoric 'this'. Austrian 'rufen' does not carry the naming sense, so the syntax/semantics outrun A1+; the gist survives but a human should confirm.

g2u03.w.sweets.note: hintDe: "Die sammelst du an Halloween im Kürbiseimer – lecker, aber schlecht für die Zähne!" — "lecker" is a bundesdeutsch marker; Austrian Standard German prefers "so gut", "köstlich" or "fein". Style only, du-form otherwise clean. Re-emitted at warn per round-4 instruction (hintDe was not inline-editable).
